3 min read
2 hours ago

--

Hash functions are everywhere. They keep your data safe with encryption, help store data in a very efficient manner, can be used to compare equality, etc.
But why do they keep popping up?
I have already covered convergent evolution of ideas. It isn’t required background, but it helps.

Hash functions are in:

• Encryption, because their properties ensure security

• Hash tables, where you hash the item you wish to store and that determines in memory where to store/find it

• File comparison, because hashing and comparing a short string of text is a lot simpler than comparing huge files

• Cryptocurrency

• Some algorithms, i.e bloom filter and hyperloglog
So what is the common denominator?
I think two uses give it away.

• Cryptography. We want a string that does not depend on the input in any way and is only one way. Hash functions do this by being uniformly random.

• HyperLogLog (which I’ve covered). This estimates the cardinality (number of distinct elements) of a set. It works by hashing each element in the set, and then counting the largest number of 1s in a row in the binary representation of the end of the string. Then the total estimated amount of elements is 2^that count.
The common denominator of when hashing is used is when it is easier to work with randomness than the data itself!
How do you prove that a cryptographic algorithm is un-bruteforceable? Well, with statistics of course. If there are 2¹²⁸ possible equally likely outcomes, no one can brute force it.
How do you estimate the number of elements in a set? Probability is designed to guess things like this!

The Galton Board may provide a beautiful illustration of this. I have spent a while extending this idea…
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Galton boardIf you have a lot of rows of pegs and drop a bunch of balls down, the resulting curve will be Gaussian.
The path of a single ball is difficult and chaotic — but the path of a lot of balls together is easy to work with! Hashing is exactly like this.

There is also an information theoretic view here.
Every hash is equally likely, by definition. This maximizes entropy. Thus, if you want to convert an object to a kind of fingerprint, it has the lowest chance of a collision. There is no remaining structure in the output. If you have two similar objects, there is nothing to glean from the hash.
Look at Shazam.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The way Shazam identifies from over 100 million songs like magic is by creating an acoustic fingerprint. It uses some specific parts of the song and hashes them. It may use instruments, frequencies/pitch, or peak intensity. This has can then be used to instantly query the database.
Think about what would happen if two songs were similar though. They would map to similar hashes and it would create hotspots. This would make search grueling and probably break everything. The reason I use music as an example is because every song is similar. The four-on-the-floor in pop/dance music..
